content: Hello! Sorry if this question sounds dumb, but I am confused on how to handle the list_head struct correctly. Let's suppose that I want to add/remove an element from the list, in C there exist functions like list_add and list_del but these are inline and no bindigs are generated. Am I supposed to write these functions my self in the helpers.rs or am I supposed to somehow use kernel::linked_list? Thank you in advance :smile:

In Rust, we don't use the C structs directly, since that would involve a lot of unsafe code. Instead we use abstractions that allow us to write safe code.

The linked list abstractions have been in review for the last months and will be getting in this cycle.

here is the lore link of the latest patch: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240814-linked-list-v5-0-f5f5e8075da0@google.com/
if you feel comfortable cherry-picking it, then you can start using it now :)

Benno Lossin said:

here is the lore link of the latest patch: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240814-linked-list-v5-0-f5f5e8075da0@google.com/
if you feel comfortable cherry-picking it, then you can start using it now :)

Awesome! Thank you for the reply! I will take the look and apply the patch.
Note the scope of that patchset only covers the case where the element of a linked list is an Arc<T>, so it may or may not be useful for you.
I still haven't found time to check out the code of the patch but I want to basically delete an element of the ThisModule.list which currently has type list_head.
that's the list_head that links all the modules together, right?
Yes
Then I'm afraid that ListArc may not work for you directly. But that patchset also has the code operating on the linked_list in unsafe code, so probably the code still could help you.

Boqun Feng said:

Then I'm afraid that ListArc may not work for you directly. But that patchset also has the code operating on the linked_list in unsafe code, so probably the code still could help you.

Checked the code of the patch, I dont see how it can help to my issue. Correct me if I am wrong but I see rust/kernel/list.rs being created but it contains an implementation of listarc and in general the patchset is more focused to support the binder implementation. Any other ideas?

It being focused on binder is no mistake, code in the kernel requires a user in order to be submitted.

It seems like what you are trying to do has not yet been needed by someone inside of the kernel tree. If I understood you correctly, you want to take an existing linked list from C and then remove an element from Rust. You will have to use unsafe functions from bindings for that purpose, since there is no abstraction for that yet.

Benno Lossin said:

It being focused on binder is no mistake, code in the kernel requires a user in order to be submitted.

It seems like what you are trying to do has not yet been needed by someone inside of the kernel tree. If I understood you correctly, you want to take an existing linked list from C and then remove an element from Rust. You will have to use unsafe functions from bindings for that purpose, since there is no abstraction for that yet.

I totaly aggree. As I also stated in the initial question, the problem is these functions are marked as inline, so bindings are not generated for these functions. Which "path" am I supposed to follow?

we have something for inline functions, take a look at the rust/helpers directory and the .c files contained within. You can add the functions that you need to call there and then they show up in bindings
(with "add" I mean, you add a wrapper function that is not inline)

Got it! I think it will work, however I face this strange error :

error[E0609]: no field `list` on type `&'static kernel::ThisModule`
  --> samples/rust/rust_print.rs:32:35
   |
32 |         unsafe { list_del(_module.list); }
   |                                   ^^^^ unknown field

impl kernel::Module for TestModule {
    fn init(_module: &'static ThisModule) -> Result<Self> {
        pr_info!("Rust module has been loaded\n");
        unsafe { list_del(_module.list); }
        Ok(TestModule)
    }
}

Have you ever faced it again? The rest of the code is correct because it compiles fine without this line.

that is probably the case, because _module has the kernel::ThisModule type and not the module type from C, take a look at the docs of ThisModule, there you should find a way to get a pointer to module.

At least this

unsafe { bindings::list_del(&mut (*module.as_ptr()).list); }

seems to compile?

Theodoros Symeonidis said:

Boqun Feng said:

Then I'm afraid that ListArc may not work for you directly. But that patchset also has the code operating on the linked_list in unsafe code, so probably the code still could help you.

Checked the code of the patch, I dont see how it can help to my issue. Correct me if I am wrong but I see rust/kernel/list.rs being created but it contains an implementation of listarc and in general the patchset is more focused to support the binder implementation. Any other ideas?

I was more thinking that the code could help you to write your own version of list_del() in Rust.
Indeed it is useful, but do you think such implementation is currently needed so that it will be added to the mainline?

It is definitely useful in the traditional sense, but that is not what is required for code to be upstreamed. All code in the kernel needs a user (ie driver), so if there is nothing that needs this in-tree, then it won't be added.
do you have a driver that you want to upstream that also needs this?

Benno Lossin said:

do you have a driver that you want to upstream that also needs this?

No I don't have any. The module I develop is for my own use. So for now there cannot be like duplicate code in the kernel, both for rust and c?
there are so-called rust reference drivers (see https://rust-for-linux.com/rust-reference-drivers). But other than that, duplicate drivers are not permitted

Benno Lossin said:

there are so-called rust reference drivers (see https://rust-for-linux.com/rust-reference-drivers). But other than that, duplicate drivers are not permitted

Okay.

Theodoros Symeonidis said:

Benno Lossin said:

do you have a driver that you want to upstream that also needs this?

No I don't have any. The module I develop is for my own use. So for now there cannot be like duplicate code in the kernel, both for rust and c?

So if it's for your own use, you could implement a list_del() in Rust, and unblock you, and then if someone else really need this function, the function will be added.
Okay! So for now there are no plans or replacing actual existing parts of the kernel with rust code?
*on

Yes and no ;-) It's OK for a driver to be rewriten in Rust, e.g. binder, and it's up to the subsystem/driver community to make the decision. But generally speaking, new driver in Rust is just more straightforward.

Maybe you could provide some more context about your question?
What about a core part and not just a driver?
Same rule
Up to the subsystem/driver community.

Dirk Behme said:

unsafe { bindings::list_del(&mut (*module.as_ptr()).list); }

@Dirk Behme Can you explain me why this maneuver is needed?

Theodoros Symeonidis said:

Dirk Behme said:

unsafe { bindings::list_del(&mut (*module.as_ptr()).list); }

Dirk Behme Can you explain me why this maneuver is needed?

Most probably @Boqun Feng or @Benno Lossin could do that in more "Rust accurate" words. But as @Benno Lossin mentioned above "_module has the kernel::ThisModule type and not the module type from C". So we are talking about two different types (structs) here. As you want to access the C structs (type) list element you need a pointer to that C struct (type) in Rust to be able to access the C structs (type) elements.
Oh, okay. I looked at the code. From what I can understand ThisModule is just a wrapper of the raw pointer, aka almost useless.
Theodoros Symeonidis has marked this topic as resolved.
